prompt: Atlas discovery assumes each service maps to exactly one Terraform module. Some services are backed by several modules (e.g. bedrock-agentcore), and today only one module's resources, source records, and entry tools show up for such a service. Fix discovery so a service backed by multiple Terraform modules surfaces ALL of them: represent the modules on DiscoveredService as a `modules` list (each entry carrying its module `name` alongside the existing sourceId/address/headings/summary); network/examples section bindings accumulate one binding per contributing module in module order (order 10, 20, …); the record carries one entry tool per module, labeled `Terraform module: <module name>` when the service has more than one; the description comes from the first module that has a summary; and one terraform-module Source is emitted per module, titled `<Service name> — <module name> Terraform Module` for a multi-module service. Single-module services must render exactly as before (plain `Terraform module` label, `<Service name> Terraform Module` title).
accept_cmd:

prompt: Confluence reference discovery supports extra instances (`extraInstances` on ConfluenceReferenceDiscoveryConfig — a separate security-policy Cloud). On that security instance the token cannot use CQL search: `/wiki/rest/api/content/search` returns 403, while plain space listing (v1 `GET /wiki/rest/api/space/{key}/content/page`, paginated via `_links.next`) still returns 200 — so no security references are discovered. Fix createConfluenceReferenceDiscovery so an EXTRA instance whose CQL search 403s falls back to listing its configured space(s) directly: listing candidates go through the same identity + doc-type admission; a successful fallback is not reported as incomplete; the 403 is remembered so that instance's CQL is not retried on later discoveries; and the space listing is fetched once per space and reused across services. The PRIMARY instance must NOT fall back — its 403 stays an honest gap (unavailable).
accept_cmd:

prompt: parseAvailabilityPage (confluenceAvailabilityProvider) expects a synthetic two-`data-table` format, but the real Confluence availability page's storage HTML is different: (1) a Legend paragraph mapping `<ac:emoticon>` glyphs to four statuses — Available, Interim Capability, Future availability (i.e. planned), Not planned; several legend glyphs share `ac:name="blue-star"` and are only distinguishable by their emoji shortname/fallback attributes; (2) a decorative at-a-glance summary table with no location header row, which must be ignored; (3) one or more matrix tables whose header row starts with "🌏 Regions" or "🖥️ Outposts" (that word decides the location kind) followed by location columns like "US-EAST-1 (North Virginia)" (label plus parenthesized description); a "Landing Zones" row that is not a service; bold colspan section-header rows like "■ Storage" that set the domain for the service rows beneath; and service rows (name cell possibly wrapped in a link) with one cell per location holding an emoticon plus an optional trailing note such as an ETA date. Update parseAvailabilityPage to parse this shape: resolve each cell's status via the legend, keep trailing cell text as the note, treat an empty cell as absent, derive the service id from the name the way identities are derived elsewhere in the repo (parenthesized acronym wins, else strip the vendor prefix and slugify), and merge a service appearing in both the Regions and Outposts tables into one record covering all locations. The `{locations, services}` output contract is unchanged.
accept_cmd:

prompt: Discovered services are missing presentation metadata. Each DiscoveredService should carry its availability domain, and service-resource derivation should project presentation fields onto the record: category (the availability domain), a status of "active", and a Terraform-module entry tool when a module is found. Also fix the availability section binding to match by the service's machine id, not its name. The entry tool links the service's Terraform module on the registry: label exactly `Terraform module`, url `https://app.terraform.io/example/registry/modules/<module address>` (the host-less address discovery already probes, e.g. example/textract/aws).

prompt: Discovery runs the full content crawl (a Terraform-registry fetch per mapped module + a Confluence body fetch per security page) as a precondition to returning ANY service — fanning out N per-item fetches before the page can paint and blocking availability behind the whole crawl. Make discovery list-only on the hot path: `discoverServiceSources`/`discoverGuardrails` must enumerate services and policies without per-item content fetches (guardrails keep the space listing but skip page bodies, so a listed guardrail's `headings` is empty; list records carry no content-derived sections or description — availability stays, since it's selector-based). Content moves to a lazy per-resource enricher, fetched only when a single resource is actually read (detail view / agent resource read): add a `createResourceContentDiscovery(deps)` port in `context-layer/src/resources/resourceContentDiscovery.ts`, where `deps` is `{ terraform: { baseUrl, token, org, moduleMap }, guardrail: { baseUrl, token }, guardrailPageIds: Map<slug, pageId> }`, exposing `sectionsFor(record, resolutionContext)` that returns the record's section bindings (`network`/`examples` from the module README; `enforced-controls`/`exceptions` from the policy page), and wire it into `getResourceContext` so section bindings still reach the service detail view. Additionally, guardrail enumeration should support scoping to a parent page's direct children: when `confluence.rootPageId` is set, list `/wiki/api/v2/pages/{rootPageId}/children?limit=250` (follow the response's `_links.next` cursor, skip pages whose `status` is not `current`) instead of scanning the whole space, and degrade to an empty list if that endpoint denies access; fall back to the whole-space listing when unset.
accept_cmd:

prompt: `tk support` currently drafts via email or Teams. Add a GitHub issue channel with the same draft-not-send contract and env-only routing (ADR 0011). `tk support github` (and a lone `--github <dest>` override, which implies the channel) opens a pre-filled `<repo>/issues/new` draft URL: title `tk support report` (+ the report summary when available), body = a markdown diagnostic summary; the full bundle still goes to clipboard + saved file. Destination comes from `TK_SUPPORT_GITHUB` or the flag: either an `owner/repo` slug (→ `https://github.com/owner/repo`) or a full http(s) repo URL (GitHub Enterprise) kept as-is; trim a trailing slash and a `.git` suffix. The draft URL must have exactly two query params (`title`, `body`) with exactly one raw `&` — encode everything else so a body containing `&`/`#` can't inject extra params. Export `githubRepoBase(dest)` and `buildGithubIssueUrl(dest, title, body)` from `src/support/send.ts`. When it opens the draft, print a line mentioning "draft a GitHub issue"; unconfigured degrades like the other channels — save + clipboard + a hint naming `TK_SUPPORT_GITHUB`, never opening `issues/new`. Update the usage string to `tk support [email|teams|github]` and cover the new channel in the interactive menu.
accept_cmd:

prompt: `tk install` auto-detect wires only the primary host and stops. Copilot CLI runs inside the VS Code integrated terminal (it inherits `TERM_PROGRAM=vscode`), so a Copilot-CLI user running `tk install` from VS Code gets the vscode shim and NO copilot hook — their sessions then run unrewritten. Fix auto mode: after wiring the detected primary tier, additively wire every OTHER present host that has an independent, non-conflicting hook config — claude-code (`~/.claude/settings.json`) and copilot-cli (`~/.copilot`), detected by their config dirs existing — printing a line like `Also wiring copilot-cli …` for each. A forced `--host X` must stay strictly single-host (no secondary wiring), and uninstall must still remove everything.

prompt: VS Code dogfood found two settings bugs (I1/I2). VS Code's `settings.json` is JSONC — comments and trailing commas are legal — but every tk reader uses strict `JSON.parse`, so a commented settings file (a) makes `readVscodeSettingsFile` return `parse_error` and `tk status`/`optimize` report the file "could not be parsed", and (b) silently aborts the shim PATH + `TK_COMPRESS_TTY` env injection into `terminal.integrated.env.*`. Make settings parsing JSONC-tolerant across all readers (comments + trailing commas parse OK; genuinely malformed JSON must still surface as `parse_error` / still be refused by apply, leaving the file untouched). When tk needs to WRITE such a file it may reformat it to strict JSON, but only after backing up: `applyCompress` keeps its existing backup flow, and `patchVscodeSettings` should return `{ reformatted, backupPath }`, writing the untouched original to `<settingsPath>.tk-backup` whenever it reformats, while preserving unrelated keys.
accept_cmd:

prompt: Two review regressions to fix. (1) `tk gain --csv --daily` passes `new Date(0)` as the window anchor, so the daily CSV buckets start at the 1970 epoch instead of the last 30 days ending today — thread the real `now` through the CSV renderer; output stays header + 30 daily bucket rows, with recorded rows landing on their real day. (2) `tk telemetry preview` bypasses the config reader: a malformed/out-of-shape config still prints a payload and exits 0. Validate the config first and exit 1 without printing any payload, exactly like `status`/`enable`/`disable` already do.
accept_cmd:
